html:
Econocom is not a sexy AI stock. That is the point.

At about €1.40 per share, the market is pricing this Belgian IT-financing, workplace-services, hardware, audiovisual, and refurbishment platform at roughly €228 million on issued shares, or closer to €205 million after the 16.2 million treasury shares held as of May 6, 2026. Against that, 2025 continuing net profit was €53.2 million, operating margin was €118.1 million, and free cash flow was €142 million. The reported 2025 net profit of only €6.4 million is the bad screen: it was crushed by the non-cash Synertrade impairment and discontinued-operations loss tied to exiting the residual software editor activity. Synertrade was sold on March 31, 2026, so the forward earnings base should be the continuing business, not the polluted statutory bottom line.

The simplest version of the thesis is that Econocom is trading at roughly 4x clean forward earnings, 2-3x normalized free cash flow, less than 0.1x revenue, and around 0.5x book, while the company has just bought back almost 10% of the issued share count near the current price. That is a very different setup from a passive net-cash value trap. The cheapness is coming from operating earnings, active capital return, and a cleaned-up structure, not from idle cash.

The market is still labeling Econocom as a low-margin European IT reseller with a complicated financing balance sheet. The business is now more interesting than that label. The three engines are complementary: Technology Management & Financing generated €1.127 billion of 2025 revenue and €60 million of operating margin; Products & Solutions generated €1.271 billion and €34 million; Services generated €526 million and €25 million. The financing arm is the profit leader, but the strategic direction is broader: full lifecycle management of digital assets, workplace, infrastructure, audiovisual integration, leasing, refurbishment, and end-of-life recovery.

The forward variant perception is that AI-driven hardware scarcity may actually help Econocom rather than simply hurt it. Q1 2026 was soft, with revenue of €679 million, flat reported and down 1.9% organically, because supply-chain pressure and geopolitics slowed demand. But management’s response is exactly where the torque is: supplier diversification, lifecycle extension, refurbished equipment, lease extension, and higher-value offers. For customers that cannot get the latest hardware cheaply or quickly, financing, refurbishing, extending, and managing digital assets becomes more valuable. Econocom is not selling AI chips; it is monetizing the messy capex cycle around digital infrastructure.

The April and May 2026 buybacks are the clearest signal. The company purchased 11 million shares on April 23-24 at about €1.47 and another 4.48 million shares on May 6 at €1.40. Including earlier purchases, treasury shares reached 16.23 million, or 9.97% of issued shares. A company buying nearly 10% of itself at 4x clean earnings is a materially accretive event. The March 2026 authorization allows repurchases up to 88 million shares between €1 and €10, so this is not just token liquidity support.

The business transition is also underappreciated. Econocom’s audiovisual acquisitions in Germany, Spain, Ireland, and the UK created a European AV integrator with over €300 million of annual revenue and 750 experts. That matters because AV, collaboration, digital signage, hybrid-work infrastructure, and managed workplace are not the same as commodity PC resale. They pull through financing, services, maintenance, logistics, and lifecycle work. The company is reorganizing around four verticals: Workplace, Audiovisual, Infrastructure, and Financing Solutions. If that structure lifts margins from the current 4% operating-margin level toward even 4.5-5.0%, the equity math changes quickly.

The retail context is also useful. This is not a crowded recovery trade. French retail forums read like capitulation: holders complain that the stock trades like a failed Belgian small cap despite revenue near €3 billion, while German quote pages show minimal watchlist interest. The stock has fallen from a 52-week high near €2.05 to the €1.40 area even after the company improved net financial debt to €36 million at year-end 2025 and began buying back stock aggressively. That is the kind of ignored, irritated shareholder base where a rerating can start from very low expectations.

The main bear argument is that margins are structurally poor. P&S is competitive, Services declined in Q1 2026 after planned contract terminations, and TMF had a tough Q1 comparison after strong 2025 momentum. Also, shareholder control is concentrated: Jean-Louis Bouchard-controlled companies hold about 55% of ownership and about 70% of voting rights, so minority shareholders do not control the value-unlock timetable. The other risk is that free cash flow can be lumpy because this is a financing and working-capital-heavy model. I would not underwrite €142 million of FCF as a permanent annual run rate.

But the stock does not need heroic assumptions. If continuing earnings remain around €55 million, the current ex-treasury equity value is below 4x earnings. If operating profit is only stable near €100 million and normalized FCF settles at €70-90 million, the equity is still priced like a shrinking distressed business, not a controlled but solvent platform buying back stock at a discount.

My base case is not a full 2028 strategic-plan success. It is simply: Synertrade noise is gone, 2026 revenue grows low single digits after a weak Q1, operating margin holds near €115-125 million, net debt remains modest after buybacks and dividends, and the market pays 7x clean earnings or roughly 5.5-6.0x operating profit. That supports about €3.60 per share.

Bear case: €1.20, down about 14% from €1.40. This assumes Q1 weakness persists, TMF remains pressured, P&S margins deteriorate, continuing net profit falls toward €35-40 million, buybacks slow, and the market keeps a distressed 4-5x earnings multiple.

Base case: €3.60, up about 157%. This assumes €55-60 million of clean net income, normalized FCF around €75 million, continued share-count reduction, and a modest rerating to still-cheap European small-cap multiples.

Bull case: €5.75, up about 311%. This assumes the One Econocom vertical strategy works, AV and financing solutions get recognized as higher-quality businesses, operating margin rises toward €135-150 million over the medium term, and the company is valued at 8x operating profit or 10-11x clean earnings after further buybacks.
